infobox.value: is the third-largest of the world's five [oceanic](/wiki/Ocean "Ocean") divisions, covering 70,560,000 km2 (27,240,000 sq mi) or approximately 20% of the water area of [Earth's surface](/wiki/Earth#Surface "Earth").[[4]](#cite_note-NOAA-volume-4) It is bounded by Asia to the north, Africa to the west and [Australia](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)") to the east. To the south it is bounded by the [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean") or [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), depending on the definition in use.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The Indian Ocean has large [marginal](/wiki/Marginal_sea "Marginal sea") or regional seas, including the [Andaman Sea](/wiki/Andaman_Sea "Andaman Sea"), the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea"), the [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal"), and the [Laccadive Sea](/wiki/Laccadive_Sea "Laccadive Sea").

The **Indian Ocean** is the third-largest of the world's five [oceanic](/wiki/Ocean "Ocean") divisions, covering 70,560,000 km2 (27,240,000 sq mi) or approximately 20% of the water area of [Earth's surface](/wiki/Earth#Surface "Earth").[[4]](#cite_note-NOAA-volume-4) It is bounded by Asia to the north, Africa to the west and [Australia](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)") to the east. To the south it is bounded by the [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean") or [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"), depending on the definition in use.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The Indian Ocean has large [marginal](/wiki/Marginal_sea "Marginal sea") or regional seas, including the [Andaman Sea](/wiki/Andaman_Sea "Andaman Sea"), the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea"), the [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal"), and the [Laccadive Sea](/wiki/Laccadive_Sea "Laccadive Sea").

Geologically, the Indian Ocean is the youngest of the oceans (being directly preceded by the [Tethys Ocean](/wiki/Tethys_Ocean "Tethys Ocean"), which fragmented into the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") and the Indian Ocean only 20 million years ago),[[6]](#cite_note-:12-6) and it has distinct features such as narrow [continental shelves](/wiki/Continental_shelf "Continental shelf"). Its average depth is 3,741 m. It is the warmest ocean, with a significant impact on [global climate](/wiki/Global_climate "Global climate") due to its interaction with the atmosphere. Its waters are affected by the Indian Ocean [Walker circulation](/wiki/Walker_circulation "Walker circulation"), resulting in unique oceanic currents and upwelling patterns. The Indian Ocean is ecologically diverse, with important ecosystems such as coral reefs, mangroves, and seagrass beds. It hosts a significant portion of the world's tuna catch and is home to endangered marine species. The climate around the Indian Ocean is characterized by [monsoons](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon").

The Indian Ocean has been a hub of cultural and commercial exchange since ancient times. It played a key role in early human migrations and the spread of civilizations. In modern times, it remains crucial for global trade, especially in oil and hydrocarbons. Environmental and geopolitical concerns in the region include [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), [overfishing](/wiki/Overfishing "Overfishing"), [pollution](/wiki/Pollution "Pollution"), piracy, and disputes over island territories.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Ocean&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The Indian Ocean has been known by its present name since at least 1515, when the Latin form *Oceanus Orientalis Indicus* (lit. 'Indian Eastern Ocean') is attested, named after India, which [projects into it](/wiki/Indian_peninsula "Indian peninsula"). It was earlier known as the *Eastern Ocean*, a term that was still in use during the mid-18th century, as opposed to the *Western Ocean* ([Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean")) before the [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") was surmised.[[7]](#cite_note-HarperIndianOcean-7) The modern [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") name for the Ocean is हिंद महासागर (*Hind Mahāsāgar*; lit. transl. Ocean of India).

Conversely, [Chinese explorers](/wiki/Ming_treasure_voyages "Ming treasure voyages") (e.g., [Zheng He](/wiki/Zheng_He "Zheng He") during the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty")) who traveled to the Indian Ocean during the 15th century called it the Western Oceans.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In [Ancient Greek geography](/wiki/List_of_Graeco-Roman_geographers "List of Graeco-Roman geographers"), the Indian Ocean region known to the Greeks was called the [Erythraean Sea](/wiki/Erythraean_Sea "Erythraean Sea").[[9]](#cite_note-Periplus-9)

In traditional South Asian geography, the ocean is historically called the "Mahodadhi", divided into the two seas Purvapayodhi (sunrise) and Apara (sunset).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Extent and data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Ocean&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Extent and data")]

The [borders of the Indian Ocean](/wiki/Borders_of_the_oceans#Indian_Ocean "Borders of the oceans"), as delineated by the [International Hydrographic Organization](/wiki/International_Hydrographic_Organization "International Hydrographic Organization") in 1953, included the [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean") but not the marginal seas along the northern rim.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In 2002 the IHO delimited the Southern Ocean separately, which removed waters south of 60°S from the Indian Ocean but included the northern marginal seas.[[12]](#cite_note-IHO-2002-12)

[Meridionally](/wiki/Meridional "Meridional"), the Indian Ocean is delimited from the Atlantic Ocean by the [20° east meridian](/wiki/20th_meridian_east "20th meridian east"), running south from [Cape Agulhas](/wiki/Cape_Agulhas "Cape Agulhas"), South Africa, and from the Pacific Ocean by the meridian of 146°49'E, running south from [South East Cape](/wiki/South_East_Cape "South East Cape") on the island of [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania") in Australia. The northernmost extent of the Indian Ocean (including marginal seas) is approximately [30°N](/wiki/30th_parallel_north "30th parallel north") in the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf").[[12]](#cite_note-IHO-2002-12)

The Indian Ocean covers 70,560,000 km2 (27,240,000 sq mi), including the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") and the Persian Gulf but excluding the Southern Ocean, or 19.5% of the world's oceans. Its volume is 264,000,000 km3 (63,000,000 cu mi) or 19.8% of the world's oceans' volume; it has an average depth of 3,741 m (12,274 ft) and a maximum depth of 7,290 m (23,920 ft).[[4]](#cite_note-NOAA-volume-4)

All of the Indian Ocean is in the [Eastern Hemisphere](/wiki/Eastern_Hemisphere "Eastern Hemisphere"). The centre of the Eastern Hemisphere, the [90th meridian east](/wiki/90th_meridian_east "90th meridian east"), passes through the [Ninety East Ridge](/wiki/Ninety_East_Ridge "Ninety East Ridge").

Within these waters are a number of islands. These include those controlled by surrounding countries, and independent island states and territories. Of the non-coastal islands, there are two broad clusters: one around Madagascar, and one south of India. A few other oceanic islands are scattered elsewhere.[[13]](#cite_note-Bergin2019-13): 29–30

### Coasts and shelves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Ocean&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Coasts and shelves")]

In contrast to the Atlantic and Pacific, the Indian Ocean is enclosed by major landmasses and an archipelago on three sides and does not stretch from pole to pole, and can be likened to an embayed ocean. It is centered on the Indian Peninsula. Although this subcontinent has played a significant role in its history, the Indian Ocean has foremostly been a cosmopolitan stage, interlinking diverse regions by innovations, trade, and religion since early in human history.[[14]](#cite_note-Prange-2008-p1382-14)

The [active margins](/wiki/Active_margin "Active margin") of the Indian Ocean have an average width (horizontal distance from land to shelf break[[15]](#cite_note-15)) of 19 ± 0.61 km (11.81 ± 0.38 mi) with a maximum width of 175 km (109 mi). The [passive margins](/wiki/Passive_margin "Passive margin") have an average width of 47.6 ± 0.8 km (29.58 ± 0.50 mi).[[16]](#cite_note-16)
The average width of the [slopes](/wiki/Continental_slope "Continental slope") (horizontal distance from shelf break to foot of slope) of the continental shelves are 50.4–52.4 km (31.3–32.6 mi) for active and passive margins respectively, with a maximum width of 205.3–255.2 km (127.6–158.6 mi).[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In correspondence of the [Shelf break](/wiki/Shelf_break "Shelf break"), also known as Hinge zone, the [Bouguer gravity](/wiki/Bouguer_gravity "Bouguer gravity") ranges from 0 to 30 [mGals](/wiki/Gal_(unit) "Gal (unit)") that is unusual for a continental region of around 16 km thick sediments. It has been hypothesized that the "Hinge zone may represent the relict of continental and proto-oceanic crustal boundary formed during the rifting of India from [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica#Geography "Antarctica")."[[18]](#cite_note-1365-246X_208_3-18)

Australia, Indonesia, and India are the three countries with the longest shorelines and [exclusive economic zones](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone "Exclusive economic zone"). The continental shelf makes up 15% of the Indian Ocean.
More than two billion people live in countries bordering the Indian Ocean, compared to 1.7 billion for the Atlantic and 2.7 billion for the Pacific (some countries border more than one ocean).[[2]](#cite_note-KeeIrv-2005-2)

#### Rivers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Ocean&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rivers")]

The Indian Ocean [drainage basin](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") covers 21,100,000 km2 (8,100,000 sq mi), virtually identical to that of the Pacific Ocean and half that of the Atlantic basin, or 30% of its ocean surface (compared to 15% for the Pacific). The Indian Ocean drainage basin is divided into roughly 800 individual basins, half that of the Pacific, of which 50% are located in Asia, 30% in Africa, and 20% in Australasia. The rivers of the Indian Ocean are shorter on average (740 km (460 mi)) than those of the other major oceans. The largest rivers are ([order 5](/wiki/Strahler_number "Strahler number")) the [Zambezi](/wiki/Zambezi "Zambezi"), [Ganges](/wiki/Ganges_River "Ganges River")-[Brahmaputra](/wiki/Brahmaputra "Brahmaputra"), [Indus](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River"), [Jubba](/wiki/Jubba_River "Jubba River"), and [Murray](/wiki/Murray_River "Murray River") rivers and (order 4) the [Shatt al-Arab](/wiki/Shatt_al-Arab "Shatt al-Arab"), [Wadi Ad Dawasir](/wiki/Wadi_Ad_Dawasir "Wadi Ad Dawasir") (a dried-out river system on the Arabian Peninsula) and [Limpopo](/wiki/Limpopo_River "Limpopo River") rivers.[[19]](#cite_note-19) After the breakup of East [Gondwana](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana") and the formation of the Himalayas, the Ganges-Brahmaputra rivers flow into the world's largest delta known as the [Bengal delta](/wiki/Bengal_delta "Bengal delta") or [Sunderbans](/wiki/Sunderbans "Sunderbans").[[18]](#cite_note-1365-246X_208_3-18)

### Marginal seas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Ocean&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Marginal seas")]

[Marginal seas](/wiki/Marginal_seas "Marginal seas"), gulfs, bays and straits of the Indian Ocean include:[[12]](#cite_note-IHO-2002-12)

Along the east coast of Africa, the [Mozambique Channel](/wiki/Mozambique_Channel "Mozambique Channel") separates [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar") from mainland Africa, while the [Sea of Zanj](/wiki/Sea_of_Zanj "Sea of Zanj") is located north of Madagascar.

On the northern coast of the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea"), [Gulf of Aden](/wiki/Gulf_of_Aden "Gulf of Aden") is connected to the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea") by the strait of [Bab-el-Mandeb](/wiki/Bab-el-Mandeb "Bab-el-Mandeb"). In the Gulf of Aden, the [Gulf of Tadjoura](/wiki/Gulf_of_Tadjoura "Gulf of Tadjoura") is located in Djibouti and the [Guardafui Channel](/wiki/Guardafui_Channel "Guardafui Channel") separates Socotra island from the Horn of Africa. The northern end of the Red Sea terminates in the [Gulf of Aqaba](/wiki/Gulf_of_Aqaba "Gulf of Aqaba") and [Gulf of Suez](/wiki/Gulf_of_Suez "Gulf of Suez"). The Indian Ocean is artificially connected to the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") without ship lock through the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal"), which is accessible via the Red Sea. The Arabian Sea is connected to the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") by the [Gulf of Oman](/wiki/Gulf_of_Oman "Gulf of Oman") and the [Strait of Hormuz](/wiki/Strait_of_Hormuz "Strait of Hormuz"). In the Persian Gulf, the [Gulf of Bahrain](/wiki/Gulf_of_Bahrain "Gulf of Bahrain") separates Qatar from the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula").

Along the west coast of India, the [Gulf of Kutch](/wiki/Gulf_of_Kutch "Gulf of Kutch") and [Gulf of Khambat](/wiki/Gulf_of_Khambat "Gulf of Khambat") are located in Gujarat in the northern end while the [Laccadive Sea](/wiki/Laccadive_Sea "Laccadive Sea") separates the Maldives from the southern tip of India. The [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal") is off the east coast of India. The [Gulf of Mannar](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mannar "Gulf of Mannar") and the [Palk Strait](/wiki/Palk_Strait "Palk Strait") separate Sri Lanka from India, while [Adam's Bridge](/wiki/Adam%27s_Bridge "Adam's Bridge") separates the two. The [Andaman Islands](/wiki/Andaman_Islands "Andaman Islands") are between the Bay of Bengal and the [Andaman Sea](/wiki/Andaman_Sea "Andaman Sea").

In Indonesia, the so-called [Indonesian Seaway](/wiki/Geology_of_Indonesia#Indonesian_Seaway "Geology of Indonesia") is composed of the [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca_Strait "Malacca Strait"), [Sunda](/wiki/Sunda_Strait "Sunda Strait") and [Torres Straits](/wiki/Torres_Strait "Torres Strait").
The [Gulf of Carpentaria](/wiki/Gulf_of_Carpentaria "Gulf of Carpentaria") is located on the Australian north coast while the [Great Australian Bight](/wiki/Great_Australian_Bight "Great Australian Bight") constitutes a large part of its southern coast.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Ocean&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Indian_Ocean_Monsoon.jpg/250px-Indian_Ocean_Monsoon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indian_Ocean_Monsoon.jpg)

During summer, warm continental masses draw moist air from the Indian Ocean, producing heavy rainfall. The process is reversed during winter, resulting in dry conditions.

Several features make the Indian Ocean unique. It constitutes the core of the large-scale [Tropical Warm Pool](/wiki/Tropical_Warm_Pool "Tropical Warm Pool") which, when interacting with the atmosphere, affects the climate both regionally and globally. Asia blocks heat export and prevents the ventilation of the Indian Ocean [thermocline](/wiki/Thermocline "Thermocline"). Asia also drives the Indian Ocean [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon"), the strongest on Earth, which causes large-scale seasonal variations in ocean currents, including the reversal of the [Somali Current](/wiki/Somali_Current "Somali Current") and [Indian Monsoon Current](/wiki/Indian_Monsoon_Current "Indian Monsoon Current"). Because of the Indian Ocean [Walker circulation](/wiki/Walker_circulation "Walker circulation"), there are no continuous equatorial easterlies. [Upwelling](/wiki/Upwelling "Upwelling") occurs near the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa") and the [Arabian Peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula") in the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") and north of the trade winds in the Southern Hemisphere. The [Indonesian Throughflow](/wiki/Indonesian_Throughflow "Indonesian Throughflow") is a unique Equatorial connection to the Pacific.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The climate north of the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") is affected by a [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") climate. Strong north-east winds blow from October until April; from May until October, south and west winds prevail. In the Arabian Sea, the violent Monsoon brings rain to the Indian subcontinent. In the southern hemisphere, the winds are generally milder, but summer storms near Mauritius can be severe. When the monsoon winds change, cyclones sometimes strike the shores of the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea") and the [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal").[[24]](#cite_note-oceanographer-2001-24)

Some 80% of the total annual rainfall in India occurs during summer and the region is so dependent on this rainfall that many civilisations perished when the Monsoon failed in the past. The huge variability in the Indian Summer Monsoon has also occurred pre-historically, with a strong, wet phase 33,500–32,500 BP; a weak, dry phase 26,000–23,500 BC; and a very weak phase 17,000–15,000 BP,
corresponding to a series of dramatic global events: [Bølling–Allerød warming](/wiki/B%C3%B8lling%E2%80%93Aller%C3%B8d_warming "Bølling–Allerød warming"), [Heinrich](/wiki/Heinrich_event "Heinrich event"), and [Younger Dryas](/wiki/Younger_Dryas "Younger Dryas").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Aerosol_pollution_over_Northern_India%2C_Bangladesh%2C_and_Bay_of_Bengal.jpg/250px-Aerosol_pollution_over_Northern_India%2C_Bangladesh%2C_and_Bay_of_Bengal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerosol_pollution_over_Northern_India,_Bangladesh,_and_Bay_of_Bengal.jpg)

Air pollution in South Asia spread over the Bay of Bengal and beyond.

The Indian Ocean is the warmest ocean in the world.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Long-term ocean temperature records show a rapid, continuous warming in the Indian Ocean, at about 1.2 °C (34.2 °F) (compared to 0.7 °C (33.3 °F) for the warm pool region) during 1901–2012.[[27]](#cite_note-:0-27) Research indicates that human induced [greenhouse warming](/wiki/Greenhouse_warming "Greenhouse warming"), and changes in the frequency and magnitude of [El Niño](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o "El Niño") (or the [Indian Ocean Dipole](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_Dipole "Indian Ocean Dipole")), events are a trigger to this strong warming in the Indian Ocean.[[27]](#cite_note-:0-27) While the Indian Ocean warmed at a rate of 1.2 °C per century between 1950–2020, climate models predict accelerated warming, at a rate of 1.7 °C–3.8 °C per century during 2020–2100.[[28]](#cite_note-:1-28)[[29]](#cite_note-:2-29)

Though the warming is basin-wide, maximum warming is in the northwestern Indian Ocean including the Arabian Sea, and reduced warming off the Sumatra and Java coasts in the southeast Indian Ocean. Global warming is projected to push the tropical Indian Ocean into a basin-wide near-permanent heatwave state by the end of the 21st century, where marine heatwaves are projected to increase from 20 days per year (during 1970–2000) to 220–250 days per year.[[28]](#cite_note-:1-28)[[29]](#cite_note-:2-29)

South of the Equator (20–5°S), the Indian Ocean is gaining heat from June to October, during the austral winter, while it is losing heat from November to March, during the austral summer.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In 1999, the [Indian Ocean Experiment](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_Experiment "Indian Ocean Experiment") showed that fossil fuel and biomass burning in South and Southeast Asia caused air pollution (also known as the [Asian brown cloud](/wiki/Asian_brown_cloud "Asian brown cloud")) that reached as far as the [Intertropical Convergence Zone](/wiki/Intertropical_Convergence_Zone "Intertropical Convergence Zone"). This pollution has implications on both a local and global scale.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Oceanography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Ocean&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Oceanography")]

Forty percent of the sediment of the Indian Ocean is found in the Indus and Ganges fans. The oceanic basins adjacent to the continental slopes mostly contain terrigenous sediments. The ocean south of the [polar front](/wiki/Polar_front "Polar front") (roughly [50° south latitude](/wiki/50th_parallel_south "50th parallel south")) is high in biologic productivity and dominated by non-stratified sediment composed mostly of [siliceous](/wiki/Siliceous "Siliceous") [oozes](/wiki/Pelagic_sediment#Oozes "Pelagic sediment"). Near the three major mid-ocean ridges the ocean floor is relatively young and therefore bare of sediment, except for the [Southwest Indian Ridge](/wiki/Southwest_Indian_Ridge "Southwest Indian Ridge") due to its ultra-slow spreading rate.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The ocean's [currents](/wiki/Ocean_current "Ocean current") are mainly controlled by the monsoon. Two large [gyres](/wiki/Gyre "Gyre"), one in the northern hemisphere flowing clockwise and one south of the equator moving anticlockwise (including the [Agulhas Current](/wiki/Agulhas_Current "Agulhas Current") and [Agulhas Return Current](/wiki/Agulhas_Return_Current "Agulhas Return Current")), constitute the dominant flow pattern. During the winter monsoon (November–February), however, circulation is reversed north of 30°S and winds are weakened during winter and the transitional periods between the monsoons.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The Indian Ocean contains the largest [submarine fans](/wiki/Submarine_fan "Submarine fan") of the world, the [Bengal Fan](/wiki/Bengal_Fan "Bengal Fan") and [Indus Fan](/wiki/Indus_Fan "Indus Fan"), and the largest areas of [slope terraces](/wiki/Marine_terrace "Marine terrace") and [rift valleys](/wiki/Rift_valley "Rift valley").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The inflow of deep water into the Indian Ocean is 11 [Sv](/wiki/Sverdrup "Sverdrup"), most of which comes from the [Circumpolar Deep Water](/wiki/Circumpolar_Deep_Water "Circumpolar Deep Water") (CDW). The CDW enters the Indian Ocean through the [Crozet](/wiki/Crozet_Islands "Crozet Islands") and [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar") basins and crosses the [Southwest Indian Ridge](/wiki/Southwest_Indian_Ridge "Southwest Indian Ridge") at 30°S. In the [Mascarene Basin](/wiki/Mascarene_Basin "Mascarene Basin") the CDW becomes a deep [western boundary current](/wiki/Western_boundary_current "Western boundary current") before it is met by a re-circulated branch of itself, the North Indian Deep Water. This mixed water partly flows north into the [Somali Basin](/w/index.php?title=Somali_Basin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Somali Basin (page does not exist)") whilst most of it flows clockwise in the Mascarene Basin where an oscillating flow is produced by [Rossby waves](/wiki/Rossby_wave#Oceanic_waves "Rossby wave").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Water circulation in the Indian Ocean is dominated by the Subtropical Anticyclonic Gyre, the eastern extension of which is blocked by the Southeast Indian Ridge and the 90°E Ridge. Madagascar and the Southwest Indian Ridge separate three cells south of Madagascar and off South Africa. [North Atlantic Deep Water](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Deep_Water "North Atlantic Deep Water") reaches into the Indian Ocean south of Africa at a depth of 2,000–3,000 m (6,600–9,800 ft) and flows north along the eastern continental slope of Africa. Deeper than NADW, [Antarctic Bottom Water](/wiki/Antarctic_Bottom_Water "Antarctic Bottom Water") flows from [Enderby Basin](/wiki/Enderby_Plain "Enderby Plain") to [Agulhas Basin](/wiki/Agulhas_Basin "Agulhas Basin") across deep channels (<4,000 m (13,000 ft)) in the Southwest Indian Ridge, from where it continues into the [Mozambique Channel](/wiki/Mozambique_Channel "Mozambique Channel") and [Prince Edward fracture zone](/wiki/Prince_Edward_fracture_zone "Prince Edward fracture zone").[[36]](#cite_note-Rogers-p5-36)

North of [20° south latitude](/wiki/20th_parallel_south "20th parallel south") the minimum surface temperature is 22 °C (72 °F), exceeding 28 °C (82 °F) to the east. Southward of [40° south latitude](/wiki/40th_parallel_south "40th parallel south"), temperatures drop quickly.[[24]](#cite_note-oceanographer-2001-24)

The [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal") contributes more than half (2,950 km3 or 710 cu mi) of the [runoff water](/wiki/Surface_runoff "Surface runoff") to the Indian Ocean. Mainly in summer, this runoff flows into the Arabian Sea but also south across the Equator where it mixes with fresher seawater from the [Indonesian Throughflow](/wiki/Indonesian_Throughflow "Indonesian Throughflow"). This mixed freshwater joins the [South Equatorial Current](/wiki/South_Equatorial_Current "South Equatorial Current") in the southern tropical Indian Ocean.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[Sea surface salinity](/wiki/Salinity#Seawater "Salinity") is highest (more than 36 [PSU](/wiki/Salinity#PSU "Salinity")) in the Arabian Sea because evaporation exceeds precipitation there. In the Southeast Arabian Sea salinity drops to less than 34 PSU. It is the lowest (c. 33 PSU) in the Bay of Bengal because of river runoff and precipitation. The Indonesian Throughflow and precipitation results in lower salinity (34 PSU) along the Sumatran west coast. Monsoonal variation results in eastward transportation of saltier water from the Arabian Sea to the Bay of Bengal from June to September and in westerly transport by the East India Coastal Current to the Arabian Sea from January to April.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

An [Indian Ocean garbage patch](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_garbage_patch "Indian Ocean garbage patch") was discovered in 2010 covering at least 5 million square kilometres (1.9 million square miles). Riding the southern [Indian Ocean Gyre](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_Gyre "Indian Ocean Gyre"), this vortex of [plastic garbage](/wiki/Plastic_pollution "Plastic pollution") constantly circulates the ocean from Australia to Africa, down the [Mozambique Channel](/wiki/Mozambique_Channel "Mozambique Channel"), and back to Australia in a period of six years, except for debris that gets indefinitely stuck in the centre of the gyre.[[39]](#cite_note-39)
The garbage patch in the Indian Ocean will, according to a 2012 study, decrease in size after several decades to vanish completely over centuries. Over several millennia, however, the global system of garbage patches will accumulate in the North Pacific.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

There are two [amphidromes](/wiki/Amphidromic_point "Amphidromic point") of opposite rotation in the Indian Ocean, probably caused by [Rossby wave](/wiki/Rossby_wave "Rossby wave") propagation.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[Icebergs](/wiki/Iceberg "Iceberg") drift as far north as [55° south latitude](/wiki/55th_parallel_south "55th parallel south"), similar to the Pacific but less than in the Atlantic where icebergs reach up to 45°S. The volume of iceberg loss in the Indian Ocean between 2004 and 2012 was 24 [Gt](/wiki/Gigatonne "Gigatonne").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Since the 1960s, [anthropogenic warming](/wiki/Anthropogenic_climate_change "Anthropogenic climate change") of the global ocean combined with contributions of freshwater from retreating land ice causes a global rise in sea level. Sea level also increases in the Indian Ocean, except in the south tropical Indian Ocean where it decreases, a pattern most likely caused by rising levels of [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas").[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Marine life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indian_Ocean&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Marine life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/CSIRO_ScienceImage_3012_Dolphin.jpg/250px-CSIRO_ScienceImage_3012_Dolphin.jpg)](/wiki/File:CSIRO_ScienceImage_3012_Dolphin.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Maldives_Surgeonfish%2C_Acanthurus_leucosternon.jpg/250px-Maldives_Surgeonfish%2C_Acanthurus_leucosternon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maldives_Surgeonfish,_Acanthurus_leucosternon.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Vagues_et_manchots_%C3%A0_l%27assaut_de_la_plage.jpg/250px-Vagues_et_manchots_%C3%A0_l%27assaut_de_la_plage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vagues_et_manchots_%C3%A0_l%27assaut_de_la_plage.jpg)

A dolphin off Western Australia and a swarm of [surgeonfish](/wiki/Acanthurus_leucosternon "Acanthurus leucosternon") near Maldives Islands represents the well-known, exotic fauna of the warmer parts of the Indian Ocean. King Penguins on a beach in the [Crozet Archipelago](/wiki/Crozet_Islands "Crozet Islands") near Antarctica attract fewer tourists.

Among the tropical oceans, the western Indian Ocean hosts one of the largest concentrations of [phytoplankton](/wiki/Phytoplankton "Phytoplankton") blooms in summer, due to the strong [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") winds. The monsoonal wind forcing leads to a strong coastal and open ocean [upwelling](/wiki/Upwelling "Upwelling"), which introduces nutrients into the upper zones where sufficient light is available for [photosynthesis](/wiki/Photosynthesis "Photosynthesis") and phytoplankton production.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

These phytoplankton blooms support the marine ecosystem, as the base of the marine food web, and eventually the larger fish species. The Indian Ocean accounts for the second-largest share of the most economically valuable [tuna](/wiki/Tuna "Tuna") catch.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Its fish are of great and growing importance to the bordering countries for domestic consumption and export. Fishing fleets from Russia, Japan, [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), and [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") also exploit the Indian Ocean, mainly for [shrimp](/wiki/Shrimp "Shrimp") and tuna.[[3]](#cite_note-CIAWFB-2018-3)

Research indicates that increasing ocean temperatures are taking a toll on the marine ecosystem. A study on the phytoplankton changes in the Indian Ocean indicates a decline of up to 20% in the marine plankton in the Indian Ocean, during the past six decades. The tuna catch rates have also declined 50–90% during the past half-century, mostly due to increased industrial fisheries, with the [ocean warming](/wiki/Ocean_warming "Ocean warming") adding further stress to the fish species.[[46]](#cite_note-46)
